title: Rosół
subtitle: Polish clear chicken broth
excerpt: A long-simmered chicken broth with root vegetables, a charred onion, and aromatic spices — the clearest, most restorative soup in Polish cooking. Served over egg noodles every Sunday.
sourceNotes: Rosół is the foundational soup of Polish home cooking — the Sunday meal across all classes and generations. Documented in Maria Lemnis and Henryk Vitry, Old Polish Traditions in the Kitchen and at the Table (1979) and Lesley Chamberlain, The Food and Cooking of Eastern Europe (1989). The charred onion is a classic technique for colour and depth.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the strained broth for up to 3 months. Defrost and reheat gently.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made the day before — the fat solidifies on top as it chills and can be lifted off easily, giving a clean, clear broth.

content.text: Rosół is not a quick soup. It is a three-hour enterprise, the broth deepening slowly over a gentle heat that must never reach a full boil. A boiling rosół turns cloudy; a trembling simmer keeps it clear. The patience is the technique.

The charred onion is a traditional step. Hold an onion half over a gas flame with tongs (or char it cut-side down in a dry frying pan) until the cut face is blackened. It goes into the broth and gives a light, caramel-brown colour and a slightly sweet depth.

items.prepNote: about 1.6 kg, cut into pieces or left whole
halved and charred over a flame or in a dry pan

content.text: Put the chicken into a large pot. Cover with 3 litres of cold water. Bring slowly to a simmer over medium-low heat — this takes about 20 minutes. As it approaches a simmer, grey foam will rise to the surface. Skim it off with a ladle, repeating until the surface is clear. Add all the vegetables, charred onion, garlic, herbs, and spices.

Cook at the gentlest possible simmer (the surface should just tremble) for 2 to 2.5 hours. Never let it boil. After 2 hours the chicken should be falling off the bone. Strain through a fine sieve into a clean pot. Season with salt. Pick the chicken meat from the bones and set aside. Slice the cooked carrots.

Cook the egg noodles separately according to the packet. Divide noodles and a few pieces of chicken and carrot between bowls. Ladle the clear hot broth over the top and scatter with freshly chopped parsley.

items.cause: Boiled at a rolling boil at some point during cooking
items.fix: Next time, hold the heat at a bare simmer; cloudy broth still tastes fine — for a clear result next time, start with cold water and never let it boil hard

items.cause: Not seasoned at the end, or too much water
items.fix: Season assertively with salt only after straining; the flavour concentrated over 2 hours needs a proper amount of salt to come through

content.text: Rosół is the Sunday soup. In Poland, Sunday lunch without rosół is almost unthinkable in the older generation. The broth comes first, served with homemade egg noodles; the boiled chicken follows as the second course. The soup has cultural weight far beyond its ingredients — it is the smell of a Polish grandmother's kitchen, the taste of recovery from illness, the meal that marks the beginning of a long family afternoon.
